prompt: What is the Standard Error of Estimate?

options.text: Tests hypotheses about population variance. Always positive, right-skewed. Degrees of freedom = n-1.
Measures dispersion of actual Y values around the regression line — lower SEE = better fit.
Error terms are correlated with each other across time — common in time series. Violates OLS independence assumption.
The value at a future date of a present amount compounded at rate r for n periods.

explanation: Measures dispersion of actual Y values around the regression line — lower SEE = better fit. Formula: $$SEE = \sqrt{\frac{SSE}{n-2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum(Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2}{n-2}}$$ Example: SEE = 3.2% → actual returns are typically within ±3.2% of predicted returns

distractorExplanations.a: Chi-square always positive. Used for variance tests and goodness-of-fit. F-distribution used for testing equality of two variances.
distractorExplanations.c: Detected by: Durbin-Watson statistic (DW ≈ 2 = no autocorrelation, DW < 2 = positive). Fix: Hansen method or include lagged variables.
distractorExplanations.d: Using simple interest instead of compound. Always compound unless stated otherwise.

prompt: What is the Sharpe Ratio?

options.text: 1) State H₀ and Hₐ. 2) Choose significance level (α). 3) Select test statistic. 4) State decision rule. 5) Calculate test statistic. 6) Make decision. 7) Interpret.
Probability of both A and B occurring.
Excess return per unit of total risk (standard deviation). Higher = better risk-adjusted performance.
Measure of asymmetry of a distribution around its mean. Positive skew: long right tail. Negative skew: long left tail.

explanation: Excess return per unit of total risk (standard deviation). Higher = better risk-adjusted performance. Formula: $$SR = \frac{\bar{R}_p - R_f}{\sigma_p}$$ Example: Portfolio return 12%, risk-free 3%, std dev 15% → SR = (12-3)/15 = 0.60

distractorExplanations.a: Never 'accept H₀' — only 'fail to reject H₀'. Type I error: reject true H₀. Type II error: fail to reject false H₀.
distractorExplanations.b: If A and B independent: P(A∩B) = P(A) × P(B). Independence means P(A|B) = P(A).
distractorExplanations.d: Symmetric distribution: skewness = 0. Negative skew = more extreme negative outcomes than positive.

prompt: How is the OLS slope coefficient calculated?

options.text: Probability of both A and B occurring.
Ratio of covariance between X and Y to variance of X.
Sum of PVs of all cash flows including initial investment. Positive NPV → project adds value.
Excess return per unit of total risk (standard deviation). Higher = better risk-adjusted performance.

explanation: Ratio of covariance between X and Y to variance of X. Formula: $$\hat{b}_1 = \frac{Cov(X,Y)}{Var(X)} = \frac{\sum(X_i-\bar{X})(Y_i-\bar{Y})}{\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2}$$ Example: Cov(X,Y) = 0.006, Var(X) = 0.004 → b₁ = 0.006/0.004 = 1.5. One unit increase in X → 1.5 unit increase in Y

distractorExplanations.a: If A and B independent: P(A∩B) = P(A) × P(B). Independence means P(A|B) = P(A).
distractorExplanations.c: NPV > 0 → accept. NPV = 0 → indifferent. NPV < 0 → reject. Do NOT confuse with IRR decision rule.
distractorExplanations.d: Sharpe uses total risk (σ). Treynor uses systematic risk (β). Information ratio uses active risk (tracking error).

prompt: What is a z-score?

options.text: Measure of asymmetry of a distribution around its mean. Positive skew: long right tail. Negative skew: long left tail.
Tests equality of two population variances. Ratio of two chi-square variables. Always positive.
Number of standard deviations an observation is from the mean. Converts any normal to standard normal (μ=0, σ=1).
Geometric mean of sub-period returns — eliminates effect of external cash flows. Best measure of manager skill.

explanation: Number of standard deviations an observation is from the mean. Converts any normal to standard normal (μ=0, σ=1). Formula: $$z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$ Example: X = 130, μ = 100, σ = 15 → z = (130-100)/15 = 2.0. Look up z-table: P(X < 130) = 97.7%

distractorExplanations.a: Symmetric distribution: skewness = 0. Negative skew = more extreme negative outcomes than positive.
distractorExplanations.b: Put larger variance in numerator → F ≥ 1. Used in ANOVA and regression F-test. Two sets of df: (n₁-1, n₂-1).
distractorExplanations.d: TWR preferred for evaluating manager performance because manager cannot control when clients invest/withdraw. MWR reflects client behavior.

prompt: What is Interest Rate Parity?

options.text: Consumer surplus: difference between willingness to pay and actual price — area above price below demand curve. Producer surplus: area below price above supply curve.
Percentage change in quantity of good A divided by percentage change in price of good B.
Forward exchange rate reflects interest rate differential. Higher interest rate currency depreciates forward (covered IRP = no arbitrage).
Nominal interest rate = real interest rate + expected inflation. Real rate roughly constant → nominal rates move with inflation expectations.

explanation: Forward exchange rate reflects interest rate differential. Higher interest rate currency depreciates forward (covered IRP = no arbitrage). Formula: $$F/S = \frac{(1+r_d)}{(1+r_f)}$$ Example: USD rate 5%, EUR rate 3%, spot USD/EUR = 1.10 → forward = 1.10 × (1.05/1.03) = 1.121. USD higher rate → USD depreciates forward

distractorExplanations.a: Total surplus = CS + PS. Deadweight loss = reduction in total surplus from market distortion (tax, monopoly, price controls).
distractorExplanations.b: Positive cross-price elasticity = substitutes. Negative = complements. Zero = independent goods.
distractorExplanations.d: Exact Fisher equation: (1+r_nom) = (1+r_real)(1+π). Approximate works when rates are small. Real rate relatively stable in long run.

explanation: Upward-sloping relationship between interest rate and output in money market equilibrium. Higher output → more money demand → higher rates. Formula: $$M/P = L(r, Y)$$ Example: Output rises → more transactions → more money demand → rates rise to clear money market → LM slopes up

prompt: What is Purchasing Power Parity?

options.text: Downward-sloping relationship between real interest rate and output in goods market equilibrium. Lower rates → more investment → higher output.
Forward exchange rate reflects interest rate differential. Higher interest rate currency depreciates forward (covered IRP = no arbitrage).
Expansion (recovery): rising output, employment, confidence. Peak: maximum output, full employment. Contraction (recession): falling output, rising unemployment. Trough: minimum output.
Absolute PPP: exchange rate should equalize price levels. Relative PPP: exchange rate change = inflation differential between countries.

explanation: Absolute PPP: exchange rate should equalize price levels. Relative PPP: exchange rate change = inflation differential between countries. Formula: $$\frac{E_1}{E_0} = \frac{(1+\pi_d)}{(1+\pi_f)}$$ Example: Country A inflation 6%, Country B 2% → Country A currency depreciates by ≈4% vs Country B. High inflation → weaker currency

distractorExplanations.a: IS = goods market equilibrium. Movement ALONG IS curve: interest rate change. SHIFT of IS curve: fiscal policy, consumer/business confidence.
distractorExplanations.b: Higher interest rate → forward DISCOUNT (not premium). Intuition: high rates compensate for expected depreciation. IRP arbitrage-free by construction.
distractorExplanations.c: Business cycle affects asset allocation. Equities: best in early expansion. Bonds: best in recession (rates falling). Commodities: best at cycle peak.
